coredata.dc:title: Cluster-bomb type magnetic biosensor for ultrasensitive detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus based on low field nuclear magnetic resonance
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dongyu
Lin, Han
Chen, Le
Wu, Yangbo
Xie, Jianjun
Shi, Xizhi
Guo, Zhiyong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel cluster-bomb type signal sensing and amplification strategy in low field nuclear magnetic resonance was proposed, and a magnetic biosensor for ultrasensitive homogeneous immunoassay of Vibrio parahaemolyticus (VP) was developed. The capture unit MGO@Ab was magnetic graphene oxide (MGO) immobilized by VP antibody (Ab) to capture VP. And, the signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab was polystyrene (PS) pellets covered by Ab to recognize VP and Gd-CQDs i.e. carbon quantum dots (CQDs) containing lots of magnetic signal labels Gd3+. In presence of VP, the immunocomplex signal unit-VP-capture unit could be formed and separated by magnetic force conveniently from the sample matrix. With the successive introduction of disulfide threitol and hydrochloric acid, signal units were cleaved and disintegrated, resulting in a homogeneous dispersion of Gd3+. Thus, cluster-bomb type dual signal amplification was achieved through increasing the amount and the dispersity of signal labels simultaneously. Under optimal experimental conditions, VP could be detected in the concentration range of 5–1.0 × 106 CFU/mL, with a limit of quantitation (LOQ) 4 CFU/mL. In addition, satisfactory selectivity, stability and reliability could be obtained. Therefore, this cluster-bomb type signal sensing and amplification strategy is powerful in designing magnetic biosensor and detecting pathogenic bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Low field nuclear magnetic resonance (LF-NMR)
Cluster-bomb type magnetic biosensor
Homogeneous immunoassay
Gadolinium-doped carbon quantum dots
Vibrio parahaemolyticus (VP)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Homogeneous immunoassay of VP by LF-NMR

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of functionalized materials. (A) SEM of MGO; (B) TEM of Gd-CQDs; (C) XPS of Gd-CQDs; (D) XRD of Gd-CQDs (curve a) and GdCl3∙6H2O (curve b), and standard card of GdCl3∙6H2O (curve c); (E) FT-IR of MGO (curve a), MGO@Ab (curve b), Gd-CQDs (curve c), and PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab (curve d); (F) SEM of PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab.

Characterization of the sensing feasibility. (A) SEM of the immunocomplex capture unit-VP-signal unit; (B) Plot of T2 of the immunocomplex and T1 of the signal unit versus magnetic separation time; (C) Effect of hydrochloric acid on T1 of the system.

Optimization of experimental conditions. Selection of (A) signal label ions; (B) ligand in the synthesis of Gd-CQDs; (C) cleavage time of Gd-CQDs from the immunocomplex by DTT; (D) the concentration of hydrochloric acid for the disintegration of Gd-CQDs; (E) incubation time of Ab and target VP; and (F) the concentration of the signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab.
(A) Relationship between ΔT1 and VP concentration; (B) Selectivity of the sensor (mixture: 1.0 × 104 CFU/mL of SA, EC, VV, LM, SM and VP.); (C) Stability of the sensor.
Cluster-bomb type signal sensing and amplification strategy.
Comparison of recently reported magnetic biosensors for the detection of pathogenic bacteria.

Detection of VP in seawater and fish samples (
x ‾
± s
, n = 5).

head.ce:title: Cluster-bomb type magnetic biosensor for ultrasensitive detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus based on low field nuclear magnetic resonance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, School of Marine Science, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, School of Marine Science, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel cluster-bomb type signal sensing and amplification strategy in low field nuclear magnetic resonance was proposed, and a magnetic biosensor for ultrasensitive homogeneous immunoassay of Vibrio parahaemolyticus (VP) was developed. The capture unit MGO@Ab was magnetic graphene oxide (MGO) immobilized by VP antibody (Ab) to capture VP. And, the signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab was polystyrene (PS) pellets covered by Ab to recognize VP and Gd-CQDs i.e. carbon quantum dots (CQDs) containing lots of magnetic signal labels Gd3+. In presence of VP, the immunocomplex signal unit-VP-capture unit could be formed and separated by magnetic force conveniently from the sample matrix. With the successive introduction of disulfide threitol and hydrochloric acid, signal units were cleaved and disintegrated, resulting in a homogeneous dispersion of Gd3+. Thus, cluster-bomb type dual signal amplification was achieved through increasing the amount and the dispersity of signal labels simultaneously. Under optimal experimental conditions, VP could be detected in the concentration range of 5–1.0 × 106 CFU/mL, with a limit of quantitation (LOQ) 4 CFU/mL. In addition, satisfactory selectivity, stability and reliability could be obtained. Therefore, this cluster-bomb type signal sensing and amplification strategy is powerful in designing magnetic biosensor and detecting pathogenic bacteria.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenic bacteria are a major problem of food safety, so sensitive, accurate, rapid, and easy field detection of them is of great public health significance [1–4]. Except conventional plate counting method, most current detection methods are based on immunoassay or nucleic acid analysis including: spectral methods such as colorimetric [5–7], fluorescent [8–10], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [11,12], surface plasmon resonance [13–15] and multiangle laser light scattering ones [16]; electrochemical methods such as electrochemical sensors [17–21], electrochemiluminescent sensors [22–24] and piezoresistive cantilever sensors [25]; mass spectrometry [26], and so on. Though being good in sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy, most of above-mentioned methods usually require cumbersome sample pretreatment to eliminate the signal interference from complex sample matrix. In comparison, magnetic materials are seldom present in sample matrix, so magnetochemical methods are uniquely advantageous in terms of interference resistance and free sample pretreatment [27–29].

Low field nuclear magnetic resonance (LF-NMR)-based magnetic biosensors, as a kind of important magnetochemical methods, have been used to detect biomolecules, pathogenic bacteria, pesticide residues, and heavy metals through two main strategies [30–32]. First, the analyte triggers a change in the amount of magnetic signal labels in the system [33]. In other words, the amount of uniform partial magnetic field generated by the magnetic signal labels changes, resulting in a change in the longitudinal relaxation time T1 or lateral relaxation time T2 of surrounding water molecules [34–36]. More analytes produce greater changes in T1 or T2, thus quantitative detection could be achieved. Second, the analyte triggers a change in the dispersion state of magnetic signal labels in the system [37]. The presence of the analyte causes the switch of magnetic labels between dispersion and aggregation state, leading to changes in the partial magnetic label concentration and field intensity, and ensuing change of T1 or T2 for quantitative detection [38–40].

Magnetic biosensing is generally carried out using homogeneous immunoassay mode in several milliliters of aqueous solutions, so the concentration of the analyte or the immunocomplex formed by the analyte is not high. Therefore, only analytes at high concentration can produce detectable signals, resulting in a low detection sensitivity of magnetic biosensors [41]. For instance, limit of quantitation (LOQ) or limit of detection (LOD) for pathogenic bacteria by magnetic biosensors are usually in the range of 50–1000 CFU/mL [42,43]. Therefore, detection protocol triggering the amount and dispersity changes of magnetic signal labels simultaneously could be appropriately designed to improve the detection sensitivity of magnetic biosensor effectively.

In this work, a cluster-bomb type signal sensing and amplification strategy with PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab as the signal unit was developed. In presence of the target Vibrio parahaemolyticus (VP), the immunocomplex capture unit-VP-signal unit could be formed and rapidly separated from complex sample matrix by magnetic force. Then, Gd-CQDs in the signal unit would be disintegrated explosively when disulfide threitol (DTT) and hydrochloric acid were introduced. In this way, a stepwise amplification chain of the signal label amount VP ∼ signal units ∼ Gd-CQDs ∼ Gd3+ could be constructed, obtaining the first signal amplification. Besides, the magnetic signal labels Gd3+ ions were transferred from insufficiently homogeneous signal units to fully homogeneous Gd3+ solutions, resulting in substantial dispersity increase and thus then achieving a second signal amplification. The cluster-bomb type magnetic biosensor has a high signal-to-noise ratio, good stability, excellent accuracy, and simple sample pretreatment, which has a great potential in the rapid field detection of pathogenic bacteria.

Detailed descriptions of reagents and materials, instruments, cultivation of VP strain, preparation of magnetic graphene oxide (MGO), and sample pretreatment and preparation can be found in the supplementary data.

About 12 mg of 1-ethyl-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and 7 mg of N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were dissolved in 10 mL of 0.5 mg/mL MGO dispersion, followed by adjusting pH = 5.0 with 0.01 mol/L hydrochloric acid and stirring for 30 min. Then, 0.1 mL of 1.5 mg/mL VP polyclonal antibody (Ab) solution was added and allowed to react for 3 h to be immobilized onto MGO by EDC/NHS coupling reaction. Subsequently, 0.2 mL of 2 wt% bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution was added to block non-specific binding sites, followed by magnetical washing. Finally, after being redispersed in 10 mL of 0.1 mol/L pH = 7.4 phosphate buffered saline (PBS), the capture unit MGO@Ab dispersion was achieved.

About 1.1 g of diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA), 0.2 g of GdCl3·6H2O and 10 mL of water were mixed, ultrasonicated, and transferred into a polytetrafluoroetylene reactor. After heated at 200 °C for 6 h, dialyzed (molecular weight cutoff: 1000 Da) with water for 12 h and lyophilized, Gd-CQDs were obtained [44,45]. Then, 1 mg of Gd-CQDs and 12 mg of L-cysteine were mixed in 10 mL of water and coupled by EDC/NHS reaction. Finally, after being dialyzed and redispersed in 5 mL of PBS, thiol-functionalized Gd-CQDs dispersion was obtained.

About 0.5 mL of 2.5 mg/mL carboxyl-functionalized polystyrene (PS) pellets and 0.2 mL of ethylenediamine-terminated polyethyleneimine (PEI) were added into 10 mL of ethanol and coupled by EDC/NHS coupling reaction, followed by centrifuging at 3405 × g, washing with ethanol and redispersing in 10 mL of PBS containing 10% N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). Then, 0.2 mL of 0.01 mol/L succinimidyl 3-(2-pyridyldithio)-propionate (SPDP) was added, followed by reacting for 1.5 h, centrifuging, washing, and redispersing in 10 mL of PBS containing 10% DMF. Subsequently, 1 mL of thiol-functionalized Gd-CQDs dispersion was added and reacted at 4 °C for 10 h. After being centrifuged, washed, and dispersed in 10 mL of water, PS@Gd-CQDs dispersion was obtained. Afterwards, 0.1 mL of 1.5 mg/mL Ab solution was added and immobilized onto PS@Gd-CQDs by EDC/NHS coupling reaction. After being blocked by BSA, centrifuged, washed, and dispersed in 10 mL of PBS, the signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab dispersion was finally obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Homogeneous immunoassay of VP by LF-NMR
ce:section.ce:para: A 0.2 mL of signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab dispersion, 0.2 mL of capture unit MGO@Ab dispersion, and 1 mL of VP sample, were mixed and incubated with shaking for 30 min. Then, the capture unit-VP-signal unit immunocomplex was magnetically separated, followed by adding 0.8 mL of 0.05 mol/L disulfide threitol (DTT) solution and reacting for 20 min. After the precipitation was removed by magnetical separation and 0.2 mL of 0.01 mol/L hydrochloric acid was added, the obtained homogeneous dispersion was ready for LF-NMR measurement.

T1 was measured at 35 °C using an Inversion Recovery (IR) pulse sequence with parameters as follows: spectrometer frequency = 19.00 MHz; sampling bandwidth = 100 kHz; regulate first data = 0.08 ms; time data = 2048, time wait = 20 s; number of time inversion = 20; number sampling = 2; regulate digital gain = 3; regulate analog gain = 15.0 dB ΔT1 was calculated according to the following formula:
ΔT1 = T1(negative) – T1(positive)
where T1 (negative) was the average T1 in the absence of VP and T1 (positive) was the average T1 in the presence of VP.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the capture unit MGO@Ab is GO simultaneously immobilized by Ab and Fe3O4 nanoparticles on the surface. Ab can capture the target VP, while Fe3O4 nanoparticles can provide rapid and convenient magnetic separation. The signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab is based on PS@PEI pellets, which are PS pellets modified by dendritic macromolecular polymer PEI. The NHS ester at one end of SPDP reacts with the primary amine group on the surface of PS@PEI pellet to form a stable amide bond, and the other end of SPDP which is a pyridine disulfide reacts with thiol-functionalized Gd-CQDs to form a disulfide bond cleavable by DTT. Thus, a great number of Gd-CQDs could be assembled onto PS@PEI pellet to form PS@Gd-CQDs through the bidirectional coupling of SPDP. Finally, the signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab is obtained by immobilizing Ab on PS@Gd-CQDs through EDC/NHS reaction.

The cluster-bomb type signal sensing and amplification strategy is as follows. In presence of the target VP, the immunocomplex capture unit-VP-signal unit is formed through antigen-antibody immune reaction, which could be magnetically separated from complicated sample matrix and excess signal units to improve the anti-interference ability effectively. When DTT is introduced, disulfide bonds are cleaved and Gd-CQDs are released into the supernatant [46], which could be easily obtained through magnetic separation. After hydrochloric acid is added into the separated supernatant, Gd-CQDs are disintegrated, and Gd3+ ions are released free to form a homogeneous solution which could produce a stable LF-NMR signal after removing the precipitation. The more the amount of VP is, the more the corresponding amount of free Gd3+ ions is, and the greater the ΔT1 generated is, whereby the quantitative detection of VP can be achieved.

The proposed signal sensing strategy has dual-order signal amplification mechanism, similar to that of a cluster-bomb. The first is the amount increase of signal labels. Each VP corresponds to many signal units, each signal unit binds many Gd-CQDs, and each Gd-CQDs contains many signal labels Gd3+. Thus, a stepwise amplification chain VP ∼ signal unit ∼ Gd-CQDs ∼ Gd3+ of signal label amount can be acquired. Released by the successive introduction of DTT and hydrochloric acid, the amount of signal labels Gd3+ increases greatly compared with Gd-CQDs, achieving the first-order signal amplification. The second is the dispersity improvement of signal labels. The signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab with a huge volume contains a great number of signal labels Gd3+, so each could affect the procession of many surrounding water molecules. However, the response signal would be still weak in case of low target concentration, due to the limited amount of the signal unit. When signal labels Gd3+ are released to be homogeneously distributed in the solution through the disintegration of the signal unit, i.e. their state is transformed from aggregation to dispersion, the number of surrounding water molecules affected by the magnetic field produced by signal labels Gd3+ greatly increases because of the far larger contact area [47]. In other words, signal labels Gd3+ are released in turn from Gd-CQDs assembled on the signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab, to Gd-CQDs dispersed homogeneously in the supernatant, and finally to homogeneous solution as free ions, resulting in a significant dispersity increment of signal labels, achieving the second-order signal amplification.

SEM (Fig. 1 A) and XRD (Fig. S1) of MGO show that, it is a few-layer GO sheet uniformly covered by Fe3O4 nanoparticles with a diameter of ∼120 nm (Fig. 1A, inset), so it could be easily attracted by a magnet to realize rapid and convenient separation (Fig. S2).

TEM of Gd-CQDs (Fig. 1B) shows that, they are uniform in particle size of ∼4 nm and dispersed well (Fig. 1B, inset), main element contents of which by XPS analysis are 49.94% C, 2.57% N, 38.27% O and 9.22% Gd (Table S1). In addition, different from the Gd 4 d peak of Gd2O3 at 142.53 eV (Fig. 1C), it is split into two peaks at 141.25 eV and 152.93 eV in case of Gd-CQDs, suggesting that Gd element in Gd-CQDs exists as Gd3+ in form of coordination bonds (Fig. 1C, inset). It is also proved by XRD (Fig. 1D), no diffraction peak of free Gd3+ is present (curve a) compared with GdCl3·6H2O (curve b) and XRD standard card of GdCl3·6H2O (curve c), meaning that Gd-CQDs are formed through stable chelation between Gd3+ and CQDs [45].

Crucial assembly steps of the capture unit MGO@Ab and the signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab were characterized by FT-IR (Fig. 1E). Absorption peaks of MGO at 586 cm−1 and 1725 cm−1 correspond to Fe–O stretching vibration and CO stretching vibration in the carboxyl group (curve a) respectively. And, the latter shifts to 1639 cm−1 (curve b), indicating that Ab is successfully assembled onto MGO to obtain the capture unit M-GO@Ab through amidation reaction between carboxyl groups on MGO and amino groups on Ab. Compared with Gd-CQDs (curve c), a distinct absorption peak at 539 cm−1 of the signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab appears (curve d), indicating that Gd-CQDs are bound to PS@PEI pellets via disulfide bonds. The average particle size of the signal unit is about 220 nm which is significantly greater than that of PS pellets 120 nm (Fig. 1F and S3), mainly due to the presence of dendritic PEI encapsulated, and Gd-CQDs and Ab assembled.

VP is cylindrical with a length of about 1–3 μm and a smooth surface. When it is present together with the capture unit and the signal unit, the immunocomplex capture unit-VP-signal unit is formed (Fig. 2 A), which can be easily used for subsequent magnetic sensing.

In order to characterize the sensing principle of this sensor, i.e., whether the immunocomplex capture unit-VP-signal unit can achieve successful separation from free signal unit, magnetic separation behaviors of the immunocomplex and the signal unit in the same magnetic field were compared (Fig. 2B). The immunocomplex capture unit-VP-signal unit could be completely attracted to the wall of the bottle (left bottle, blue dashed box) within 5 min due to the presence of Fe3O4 nanoparticles in the capture unit, while free signal unit could not at all (right bottle). T2 of the immunocomplex dispersion affected by Fe3O4 nanoparticles therein is 346 ms, while that of the residual dispersion after magnetical separation increases to 2256 ms which is nearly equal to T2 of pure water 2260 ms, demonstrating that the immunocomplex is thoroughly moved out without any remnant. In contrast, T1 of the signal unit affected by Gd3+ therein is stable at about 356 ms in 30 min without any significant variation, indicating that the signal unit is stable enough without leakage of the signal label Gd3+. Therefore, the immunocomplex and free signal unit can be completely separated under the action of the magnetic field within 5 min, realizing reliable sensing of the target VP.

The displacement efficiency of H+ towards Gd3+ in Gd-CQDs was tested. If 1 mL of 0.01 mol/L hydrochloric acid solution was added into 2 mL of 1 mg/mL milk-white Gd-CQDs dispersion, it became clear and transparent instantly (Fig. 2C inset, SI video). When Gd-CQDs dispersion without adding hydrochloric acid was dialyzed, T1 of the dispersion inside and outside the dialysis bag was 360 ms and 2911 ms respectively. In case that Gd-CQDs dispersion was mixed with hydrochloric acid followed by multiple dialysis, T1 of the dispersion inside and outside the dialysis bag was 2914 ms and 2908 ms respectively. And, T1 of pure water under this condition was 2917 ms. It indicates that in absence of hydrochloric acid, Gd3+ is stable in Gd-CQDs without leakage; while in presence of hydrochloric acid, H+ can completely displace all Gd3+ in Gd-CQDs, ensuring the stability, reproducibility, and accuracy of quantitative detection.

Experimental conditions were optimized, 1.0 × 104 CFU/mL VP standard solution was used if required. As shown, signal label Gd3+ (Fig. 3 A), ligand in the synthesis of Gd-CQDs diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) (Fig. 3B), cleavage time of Gd-CQDs from the immunocomplex by DTT 20 min (Fig. 3C), the concentration of hydrochloric acid for the disintegration of Gd-CQDs 0.01 mol/L (Fig. 3D), incubation time of Ab and target VP 30 min (Fig. 3E), and the concentration of the signal unit PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab 125 μg/mL (Fig. 3F), should be selected.

The cluster-bomb type signal amplification effectiveness of the sensor was checked. As shown in Fig. 4 A, if Gd-CQDs@Ab is directly used as the signal unit, the linear regression equation between ΔT1 (y, ms) and the logarithm of VP concentration (x, CFU/mL) is y = 69.04 × logx - 121.95 over the range of 1.0 × 103–1.0 × 106 CFU/mL, with a square correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.958. The limit of quantitation (LOQ) is 1000 CFU/mL, based on the signal-to-noise ratio equal to 10. If PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab is used as the signal unit without the introduction of hydrochloric acid, the linear regression equation is y = 397.45 × logx - 535.41 over the range of 1.5 × 101–1.0 × 106 CFU/mL, with R 2 = 0.995 and LOQ 14 CFU/mL. Apparently, the sensitivity improvement is mainly attributed to the amount increment of signal labels, which is the first-order signal amplification. If PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab is used as the signal unit followed by the addition of hydrochloric acid, the linear regression equation is y = 410.63 × logx - 117.32 over the range of 0.5 × 10–1.0 × 106 CFU/mL, with R 2 = 0.985 and LOQ 4 CFU/mL. Because the addition of hydrochloric acid makes Gd3+ in Gd-CQDs completely released free through ion displacement by H+ in the form of cluster-bomb, a homogeneous Gd3+ solution and ensuing homogeneous immunoassay is achieved. In this case, the procession of more water molecules is affected, resulting in a more pronounced ΔT1, so the second-order signal amplification is obtained. Compared with recently reported ones for the detection of pathogenic bacteria (Table 1 ) [41,42,48–53], this magnetic biosensor has higher sensitivity due to the cluster-bomb type signal sensing and amplification strategy. In addition, it could be found that the amount increment of signal labels plays a greater role in sensitivity improvement than the dispersity increment of signal labels in this detection protocol, suggesting the main direction of further detection sensitivity improvement in the future.

The selectivity of the sensor was investigated with standard solutions of VP and other common pathogenic bacteria. Are shown in Fig. 4B, ΔT1 of 1.0 × 104 CFU/mL Staphylococcus aureus (SA), Escherichia coli (EC), Vibrio vulnificus (VV), Listeria monocytogenes (LM), and Salmonella (SM) were weak, are not significantly different from that of the blank. In contrast, ΔT1 of 1.0 × 104 CFU/mL VP is almost same as that of the mixed sample containing 1.0 × 104 CFU/mL of SA, EC, VV, LM, SM and VP, indicating that the sensor has satisfactory selectivity. Attributing to the specific recognition and capture ability of Ab on the signal unit and the capture unit, the other bacteria could not produce a detectable response signal.

The stability of the sensor was examined by measuring 1.0 × 104 CFU/mL VP standard solution for one consecutive week, using the same batch of capture unit and signal unit. As shown in Fig. 4C, the 7-day average value of ΔT1 is 1274 ms with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.56%, exhibiting excellent intra-day and inter-day stability. It could be attributed to the fact that, the magnetic signal intensity depends on the Gd3+ concentration which has absolute homogeneity in the aqueous phase, avoiding possible agglomeration or sedimentation of the signal unit based on magnetic nanoparticles.

The standard addition recovery experiment for VP spiked in real seawater and fish samples were performed, with the sample pretreatment method detailed in the supplementary data [54,55]. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of seawater samples are 99.3%–106.2% with RSDs 2.3%–6.5%. As for fish samples, the recoveries are 98.4%–104.9% with RSDs 1.8%–9.2%. Excellent precision and accuracy were obtained, benefitting from that the response signal is originated from uniformly dispersed ions instead of unevenly dispersed nanoparticles. In this case, undesired but unavoidable agglomeration and sedimentation could be eliminated to obtain a homogeneous immunoassay. An inter-group t-test was carried out between plate-counting method and this one, the calculated t-values for seawater and fish samples were 1.02 and 1.10 respectively, both less than t 0.05,3 = 3.18, indicating that there is no significant difference between the two methods. Therefore, it is precise, accurate, and reliable for VP detection in real samples.

In summary, a LF-NMR magnetic biosensor basing on a cluster-bomb type signal sensing and amplification strategy was developed. The nanocomposite PS@Gd-CQDs@Ab was used as the signal unit, the release and disintegration of which would increase the amount and dispersity of signal labels simultaneously, acquiring a dual sensitivity improvement. And, originated from the signal labels magnetic ions packed in the signal unit and then released to form homogeneous solution, the switch of the response signal is proposed first time in the area of LF-NMR magnetic biosensors, substantially improving the detection sensitivity, stability, and convenience of the system. By replacing the corresponding antibodies, it could become a versatile plateau promising in the field detection of pathogenic bacteria.

Dongyu Zhang: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Han Lin: Data curation, Formal analysis. Le Chen: Data curation. Yangbo Wu: Resources, Instrument system. Jianjun Xie: Supervision, Formal analysis. Xizhi Shi: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Zhiyong Guo: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (42076193), Key R&D Program of Zhejiang (2022C02028) are gratefully acknowledged.
